does: This paper evaluates language model agents as tools for autonomous red-teaming and adversarial cybersecurity operations. The authors develop a controlled evaluation framework based on MITRE ATT&CK that benchmarks LMA capabilities across two lateral-movement scenarios in an instrumented cyber environment. They formalize attack tasks as ordered chains with explicit validation predicates and employ an LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm for deterministic outcome verification. The study compares three operational modalities: fully autonomous execution, self-scaffolded planning, and expert-defined action plans, measuring how effectively LMAs can observe execution artifacts, adapt iteratively based on environmental feedback, and execute multi-step offensive procedures including attack planning and adversary emulation.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into LLM-based agents and their application to cybersecurity. It extends prior work on adversarial machine learning and red-teaming methodologies by grounding them in realistic cyber defense frameworks. The paper responds to growing concerns about autonomous AI capabilities in offensive contexts by providing structured empirical analysis rather than speculation. It connects to earlier red-teaming practices and adversary emulation traditions while addressing governance questions about responsible evaluation. The use of MITRE ATT&CK grounds the work in established cybersecurity threat modeling, and the LLM-as-Judge approach builds on recent advances in using language models for evaluation and verification tasks across domains.
enables: This work establishes empirical foundations for understanding both capabilities and limitations of autonomous agents in cybersecurity contexts, informing future governance frameworks and safety guidelines for LMA development. The controlled evaluation methodology could extend to assessing other emerging AI-based offensive capabilities and attack scenarios beyond lateral movement. The comparative analysis of operational modalities provides insights for designing LMAs with appropriate autonomy levels and safety constraints. This research enables more targeted defenses by clarifying which adversarial techniques LMAs can effectively execute and where they fail, supporting development of detection mechanisms. It also points toward better evaluation paradigms for high-stakes AI applications where deterministic verification and interpretability are critical.
